rulings.Calendar Matter: To Compel Production Of Documents; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 16, 2025, line 1, PETITIONER ANDREW LINTON'S Motion To Compel Production Of Documents (tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) The motion to compel production of documents on petitioner Andrew Linton's subpoena is denied. Linton has filed a petition for purposes of conducting discovery in aid of enforcing a federal judgment. He has not served a summons or the petition on any party. He now moves to enforce his deposition subpoena for the production of documents against the Law Offices of Mark Romeo. Romeo opposes the motion. The Sister State Money Judgments Act does not apply to federal judgments. (See Code Civ. Proc. 1710.10, subd. (c), & comment; see also Rutter Group Cal. Prac. Guide Enf. J. & Debt Ch. 6J-2 ["The SSMJA does not apply to federal court judgments. . . . (Instead, nearly all federal court judgments are enforceable by registration in a California federal district . . . .)"].) Linton does not make clear the source of law that he contends authorizes him to enforce his federal judgment in this case. Romeo's opposition cites cases for the proposition that federal courts have the jurisdiction to enforce their own judgments (which this court does not doubt) but then says conclusorily that federal courts' ancillary jurisdiction to enforce their own judgments is exclusive. That is a different proposition for which Romeo does not present direct authority. Romeo also contends that bankruptcy courts have exclusive jurisdiction over bankruptcy matters. This is also a proposition this court readily accepts, but Romeo does not explain the relationship between this judgment and the bankruptcy proceedings from which the judgment arises. The court looks forward to more extensive future briefing should this issue recur. Regardless, the court denies the motion. The subpoena seeks records of a consumer and was not served in the manner required by Code of Civil Procedure section 1985.3. Linton acknowledges as much and purports to have cured the issue on reply. But a party must present its supporting evidence in its moving papers, not on reply. Moreover, a defect of service means that objections to the deposition subpoena are not waived, yet Linton's motion is premised on the waiver of objections. Romeo contends that the subpoena has other procedural defects. The court does not reach all of them because it concludes that the failure of consumer notice alone requires denying the motion. (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM L. HELTON VS. HYUNDAI MOTOR AMERICA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 16, 2025, line 9, 4 - PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One (Tentative ruling part 1 of 2) On April 1, 2025, the court ordered the parties to meet and confer and to file supplemental briefs describing any remaining discovery dispute. Plaintiff William Helton's supplemental counsel's declaration seeks further relief in two respects: First, as to all RFPs other than 30 and 31, the Schmitt Declaration states that the parties have agreed to production parameters pending a protective order, which the parties have not yet negotiated. The court orders Hyundai Motor America to produce the documents it has agreed to produce within 20 days of entry of this order. As to any documents Hyundai contends are confidential, it may designate them as confidential in its production. Helton may challenge any confidentiality designation; the parties must meet and confer and the court will resolve any disputes about confidentiality designations upon noticed motion. Helton shall not file any of Hyundai's confidential documents in the public record or use any confidential documents in open court absent leave of the court but may lodge these documents under seal and notify Hyundai so it may make any appropriate sealing motion. Helton shall not share confidential documents outside of its law firm except with the plaintiff himself and any retained experts or consultants, who shall agree to bound by the terms of this protective order before they receive confidential documents. Helton shall return or destroy confidential documents within 60 days of the final conclusion of this litigation. Helton further seeks an order compelling Hyundai to produce documents responsive to RFP Nos. 30 and 31. Hyundai has not submitted a supplemental brief but briefed this issue extensively in its March 18, 2025 opposition to the motion. The court now turns to this issue. RFP 30 seeks a list or compilation of other customer complaints that are "substantially similar" to complaints made by Helton about his 2023 Hyundai Santa Cruz vehicle. A very broad definition of substantial similarity is given. RFP 31 seeks "all documents that refer to, reflect, or relate to any Field Service Action issued, or in the process of being issued, in response to complaints experienced by Plaintiff as described in Defendant's warranty history/summary and within the line items of the repair orders created at Defendant's authorized repair facility" [some capitalizations omitted]. The term Field Service Action is not defined. Hyundai objected to both RFPs as unintelligible, overbroad, and irrelevant. This is a Song-Beverly Act case where Helton is seeking replacement value, damages related to his repair of and inability to use the vehicle, attorneys' fees, and civil penalties for willful violations of the Act. Hyundai's knowledge of the same defects in other 2023 Hyundai Santa Cruz vehicles is relevant to whether any violations here were willful. But Helton's requests are broad and not wholly clear, and Hyundai presents unrebutted evidence that the burden of responding to these RFPs as they are drafted would far exceed their evidentiary value in this case. (Blackburn Dec. paras. 19-30.) The court reforms RFP 30 as follows: Hyundai shall provide a list of instances where a consumer presented a 2023 Hyundai Santa Cruz for repair in California where the vehicle had a seatbelt that failed to latch, a Tonneau cover that failed to lock, or a "Driver Seat Unbuckled" alert that activated when that seatbelt was buckled. Hyundai may redact consumer names for privacy. (End of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM L. HELTON VS. HYUNDAI MOTOR AMERICA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 16, 2025, line 9, 4 - PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2) The court acknowledges that Helton's Complaint identifies additional problems with his car, but these are described in such a general fashion ("abnormal noise," "oil leak," and "gears slipping") that an electronic database search for complaints with those features may or may not reveal other cars with the same defects as Helton's car. This limitation is without prejudice to Helton's propounding more specific interrogatories that are based on actual defects with the car and not merely general descriptions of complaints. The court denies relief as to RFP 31. Hyundai objected that it was unintelligible, Helton does not explain with more precision what is being sought anywhere in his moving papers, and the court does not understand the request. This denial is without prejudice to Helton's serving a new and more targeted RFP. The court appreciates the parties' efforts in narrowing their discovery disputes. The court finds that both parties acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (End of Tentative Ruling part 2 of 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. DANIEL P MCBRIDE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem Requests For Admissions Admitted And Of Nonappearance
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 16, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S Motion To Deem Requests For Admissions Admitted And Of Nonappearance The unopposed motion by plaintiff Wells Fargo Bank N.A. to deem admitted the truth of any matters specified in its Requests for Admission, Set One, is granted. Defendant Daniel McBride failed to timely respond to this discovery and has thereby waived objections. (CCP 2033.280(a).) The truth of any matters specified in the RFAs, Set One, is deemed admitted by McBride. (CCP 2033.280(b), (c).) Defendants do not seek sanctions, but CCP 2033.280(c) provides they are mandatory in this instance. The court orders McBride to $50 sanctions to Wells Fargo within 15 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address.

rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash Service Of Summons; Declaration Of Martin Mclaughlin In Support [Hearing: May 16, 2025, Dept. 301]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Friday, May 16, 2025, line 7, DEFENDANT MARTIN MCLAUGHLIN'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons The unopposed motion to quash service of summons is granted. Specially appearing defendant provides a declaration that he was not personally served and does not match the physical description provided in the proof of service of summons. "When a defendant challenges the court's personal jurisdiction on the ground of improper service of process, the burden is on the plaintiff to prove the existence of jurisdiction by proving, inter alia, the facts requisite to an effective service." (Summers v. McClanahan (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 403, 413 [internal quotation marks, citation, and footnote omitted].) Plaintiff presents no evidence to meet that burden. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)
